title: Guideline on Data Breach Notification 2025
summary: This Guideline provides comprehensive guidance on the mandatory data breach notification obligations introduced by the Personal Data Protection (Amendment) Act 2024 (Act A1699) through the insertion of section 143A into the PDPA. All data users are required to notify the Commissioner of a notifiable data breach within 72 hours of becoming aware of it, and in certain circumstances, to notify affected data subjects within 7 days. Failure to comply is an offence under s143A(5). The Guideline aligns Malaysia's breach notification framework with international standards, including the EU GDPR's breach notification requirements.

provisions.title: Definition of a Personal Data Breach
provisions.content: A personal data breach means a breach of security leading to the accidental or unlawful destruction, loss, alteration, unauthorized disclosure of, or access to, personal data transmitted, stored, or otherwise processed. This includes: (a) confidentiality breaches — unauthorized or accidental disclosure of, or access to, personal data; (b) availability breaches — accidental or unauthorized loss of access to, or destruction of, personal data; and (c) integrity breaches — unauthorized or accidental alteration of personal data. Breaches can be caused by external attackers (hacking, phishing), internal actors (unauthorized access by employees), technical failures (accidental deletion, system errors), or physical incidents (theft of devices, natural disasters affecting data centers).
requirements: Data users must maintain security measures sufficient to detect breaches promptly
Security incident logging is required to establish the timeline of a breach
All security incidents must be assessed to determine whether they constitute a personal data breach
The assessment must be documented regardless of whether the breach is notifiable

provisions.title: What Constitutes a Notifiable Breach
provisions.content: Not all personal data breaches are notifiable. A breach is notifiable when it is likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects. The Commissioner must be notified where any of the following applies: (a) the breach involves sensitive personal data (health, financial, identity documents); (b) the breach involves the personal data of more than 200 individuals; (c) the breach is likely to result in discrimination, financial loss, identity theft, reputational damage, or significant social harm to data subjects; (d) the breach involves personal data of vulnerable individuals including children, persons with disabilities, or the elderly; or (e) the breach involves access credentials (usernames, passwords, PINs) that may be used to access other systems. Breaches that are unlikely to result in any risk to data subjects (e.g., loss of properly encrypted data where the key is not compromised) are not notifiable.
requirements: A risk assessment must be conducted for every personal data breach
The risk assessment must be documented and retained for at least 5 years
Where there is doubt about notifiability, the Commissioner recommends notifying rather than not notifying
The decision not to notify must be clearly documented with reasoning

provisions.title: Notification to the Commissioner — 72-Hour Requirement
provisions.content: Where a data user has become aware of a notifiable data breach, it must notify the Commissioner within 72 hours of becoming so aware. The 72-hour clock starts when the data user has sufficient certainty that a breach has occurred and has a reasonable understanding of its nature and likely impact. Where full information is not available within 72 hours, an initial notification must be made with the information available, to be supplemented as further information becomes available. 'Becoming aware' means when the data user has reasonable certainty — a mere suspicion or preliminary indication does not trigger the clock, but a data user cannot unreasonably delay investigation to defer the start of the clock.
requirements: 72-hour clock starts from when the data user 'becomes aware' — not from the date of the breach itself
Initial notification may be made with incomplete information — a 'phased' notification is acceptable
Subsequent information must be provided to the Commissioner as it becomes available
Reasons for any delay beyond 72 hours must be documented and communicated to the Commissioner

timeline.trigger: Data user becomes aware of notifiable breach
timeline.followUp: As soon as practicable if full information not available in initial notification

provisions.title: Content of Notification to Commissioner
provisions.content: The notification to the Commissioner must be submitted using the prescribed form on the Commissioner's online portal and must contain the following information: (a) the nature of the personal data breach including the categories and approximate number of data subjects concerned, and the categories and approximate number of personal data records concerned; (b) the name and contact details of the data protection officer or other contact point where more information can be obtained; (c) a description of the likely consequences of the personal data breach; (d) a description of the measures taken or proposed to be taken to address the breach, including, where appropriate, measures to mitigate its possible adverse effects; (e) the date and time (if known) of the breach; and (f) the type of breach (confidentiality, availability, integrity, or combination).
requirements: Notification must be submitted using the prescribed form available on the JPDP website
All mandatory fields must be completed — partial submissions will not be accepted
Supporting evidence (logs, forensic reports) should be attached where available
A senior officer of the data user must certify the accuracy of the notification

provisions.title: Notification to Data Subjects — 7-Day Requirement
provisions.content: Where a personal data breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of data subjects, the data user must communicate the breach to the affected data subjects without undue delay and in any case within 7 days of determining that the data subjects must be notified. Notification to data subjects is required where the breach is likely to result in: (a) significant financial harm (e.g., fraud, identity theft); (b) significant reputational harm; (c) physical harm or risk to safety; (d) discrimination or prejudice; or (e) significant distress or psychological harm. Notification to data subjects is not required where: the data was encrypted and the key is not compromised; the data user has taken subsequent measures that eliminate the high risk; or the Commissioner determines that individual notification would involve disproportionate effort, in which case a public communication may be substituted.
requirements: 7-day clock for data subject notification starts from the determination that notification is required
The determination itself must be made promptly — it cannot be deferred indefinitely
Notification must be in clear, plain language accessible to the data subjects concerned
Notification must be in Bahasa Malaysia or English, or both, as appropriate to the data subjects

timeline.trigger: Data user determines that data subject notification is required

provisions.title: Content of Notification to Data Subjects
provisions.content: The notification to affected data subjects must include: (a) a clear description of the nature of the breach in plain language; (b) the name and contact details of the DPO or other data protection contact; (c) a description of the likely consequences of the breach for the data subject; (d) the steps the data user has taken or proposes to take to address the breach; (e) specific protective measures the data subject can take (e.g., changing passwords, monitoring accounts, placing a fraud alert); (f) where available, the types of personal data affected; and (g) a contact channel for data subjects to obtain further information. Notification must not be misleading, minimizing, or overly technical. Data users should make reasonable efforts to provide notifications in languages understood by affected data subjects.
requirements: Notification must be direct (email, SMS, post, or phone) to affected individuals where contact details are available
Website notice alone is insufficient where direct communication is reasonably practicable
Notification must not inadvertently cause additional harm (e.g., by revealing sensitive information to third parties)
The adequacy of protective measures recommended to data subjects must be appropriate to the nature of the breach

provisions.title: Record-Keeping Requirements
provisions.content: Data users must maintain a Data Breach Register documenting all personal data breaches, whether notifiable or not. The Register must record: (a) the date and time of discovery of the breach; (b) the date and time of the breach (if known); (c) the nature of the breach; (d) the personal data affected (categories and volume); (e) the likely consequences; (f) the remedial action taken; (g) whether the breach was assessed as notifiable; (h) if notifiable, the date of notification to the Commissioner and data subjects; and (i) the reasons for any decision not to notify. The Register must be retained for at least 5 years and made available to the Commissioner on request.
requirements: All breaches must be recorded — not only notifiable ones
The Register must be maintained even for minor or low-risk breaches
5-year minimum retention for all breach records
The Register must be produced to the Commissioner within 5 working days of request

provisions.title: Post-Breach Remediation and Review
provisions.content: Following a personal data breach, the data user must: (a) implement immediate containment measures to prevent further access, exfiltration, or compromise; (b) conduct a root cause analysis to identify the technical or organizational failure that led to the breach; (c) implement remedial measures to address the root cause and prevent recurrence; (d) review and update security measures, policies, and procedures as necessary; (e) conduct staff training where human error was a contributing factor; and (f) report the outcome of the post-breach review to the Commissioner within 60 days of the initial notification. The Commissioner may require a follow-up audit to verify that remediation has been effective.
requirements: Containment must be initiated immediately — remediation cannot be deferred pending notification
Root cause analysis report must be submitted to the Commissioner within 60 days
Remediation must address systemic issues, not just the immediate incident
Follow-up audit may be conducted by the Commissioner at any time after the breach

timelines.72HourNotificationToCommissioner: 72 hours from data user becoming aware of a notifiable breach
timelines.7DayNotificationToDataSubjects: 7 days from determination that data subject notification is required
timelines.60DayPostBreachReport: 60 days from initial notification to Commissioner
timelines.5YearRetention: Data Breach Register retained for minimum 5 years
timelines.5WorkingDayRecordProduction: Breach records must be produced to Commissioner within 5 working days of request
